sections.heading: Confidentiality
sections.title: Confidentiality obligations for financial institution personnel
sections.summary: Financial institution personnel and service providers must take specific confidentiality oaths or declarations before handling institution data. Directors and senior officers must take an oath of confidentiality in the First Schedule, non-resident directors must take an oath before a competent court, and others must make a declaration to the institution's CEO. Service providers with written undertakings from employees may be exempt. The duty prohibits disclosing customer information without consent, with limited exceptions including bankruptcy, legal proceedings, credit reporting, and regulatory requirements. Penalties include fines up to 1 million rupees and imprisonment for up to three years. Financial institutions must obtain prior written authorization from the central bank before disclosing confidential information to third parties. This includes individuals or entities conducting due diligence for shareholding acquisition. The disclosure must comply with confidentiality duties and any conditions set by the central bank.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires strict confidentiality protocols for cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions, with specific obligations for personnel and service providers. Mandates that customer data must not be disclosed without consent, with limited exceptions for regulatory compliance and legal proceedings. Critical for DTIA as it defines when data can be shared internationally without violating confidentiality. Requires prior central bank approval for sharing confidential financial institution information with third parties, including due diligence activities. This impacts cross-border data

sections.heading: Interpretation
sections.title: Employee transfer upon banking undertaking transfer
sections.summary: Employees of the transferor bank who are transferred to the transferee bank must receive terms and conditions not less favourable than their previous employment. Their period of service is deemed unbroken with the transferee bank, and they cannot claim termination or adverse effects due to the transfer. Pension schemes operated by the transferor bank for employees on the day before the appointed day are transferred to the transferee bank.

requirements.text: Every person employed by the transferor bank on the day immediately before the appointed day shall be entitled to be transferred to the transferee bank on terms and conditions not less favourable than those of his previous employment
The period of service of every person employed by the transferor bank who is transferred to the transferee bank shall be deemed to be an unbroken period of service with the transferee bank
No person employed by the transferor bank shall be entitled to claim that his employment has been terminated or adversely affected in breach of any enactment due to transfer to the transferee bank

Any pension or similar scheme operated by the transferor bank for the benefit of its employees on the day immediately before the appointed day shall be operated by the transferee bank as from the appointed day

sections.source_quote: 2. (a) Every person who, in relation to the transferred undertaking, on the day immediately before the appointed day, is employed by the transferor bank shall be entitled to be transferred to the transferee bank on terms and conditions which shall be not less favourable than those of his previous employment at the transferor bank. (b) The period of service of every person employed by the transferor bank who is transferred to the transferee bank shall be deemed to be an unbroken period of service with the transferee bank. (c) No person employed by the transferor bank shall, on account of his transfer to the transferee bank or any resulting change in his job title, be entitled to claim that his employment has been terminated or adversely affected in breach of any enactment. (d) Any pension or similar scheme operated by the transferor bank for the benefit of its employees on the day immediately before the appointed day shall, as from the appointed day, be operated by the transferee bank.

sections.heading: Failure to maintain minimum holdings
sections.title: Bank liquidity compliance reporting
sections.summary: Banks must provide the central bank with information within 7 days of request to confirm compliance with minimum holding requirements. The central bank determines the specific liquidity thresholds under section 22. Banks cannot increase loans, overdrafts, or investments while their liquid assets fall below the central bank's mandated level.
sections.dtia_summary: Banks must maintain minimum liquidity levels to avoid cross-border financial instability; this affects data transfer safeguards for liquidity-related financial data.

requirements.text: Banks must provide central bank with compliance information within 7 days of request
Banks cannot allow liquid assets below central bank-determined threshold
Banks cannot increase loans, overdrafts or investments while liquid assets fall below threshold

sections.source_quote: Every bank shall furnish, within a reasonable time and in any event not later than 7 days from the date of a request to that effect made by the central bank, such information as may be required by the central bank to indicate whether the bank has complied with section 22.

sections.heading: Minimum capital requirements
sections.title: Cash dealer capital maintenance
sections.summary: Cash dealers must maintain minimum capital as approved by the central bank after accounting for accumulated losses. They cannot pay dividends or make profit transfers until their capital meets the minimum requirement and adequate provisions for liabilities are made.
sections.dtia_summary: Cash dealers must maintain sufficient capital to ensure financial stability before transferring funds; impacts data transfer risk assessments for financial institutions.

requirements.text: Cash dealers must maintain minimum capital approved by central bank after deducting accumulated losses
Cash dealers cannot declare dividends until minimum capital requirement is met
Cash dealers cannot make profit transfers until adequate provisions for liabilities are made

sections.source_quote: Every cash dealer shall, at all times, maintain in Mauritius such amount paid as stated capital as may be approved by the central bank or such higher amount as may be prescribed, after deduction of accumulated losses of the cash dealer.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential financial governance

sections.heading: Minimum liquid assets of foreign exchange dealers
sections.title: Foreign exchange dealer liquidity minimums
sections.summary: Foreign exchange dealers must maintain minimum liquid assets equivalent to at least 10% of their liabilities, as determined by the central bank. This ensures liquidity for foreign exchange operations and prevents insolvency risks.
sections.dtia_summary: Foreign exchange dealers require minimum liquidity reserves; affects data transfer risk assessments for cross-border financial transactions.

requirements.text: Foreign exchange dealers must maintain minimum liquid assets equivalent to at least 10% of liabilities

sections.source_quote: Every foreign exchange dealer shall at all times maintain such minimum liquid assets, equivalent to not less than 10 per cent of its liabilities, as may be determined by the central bank.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure financial liquidity requirement, no data protection link

sections.heading: Other prudential requirements
sections.title: Cash dealer prudential compliance
sections.summary: Cash dealers must comply with sections 7 and 10 of the Act and any other prudential requirements specified by the central bank. This ensures cash dealers meet broader financial stability standards.
sections.dtia_summary: Cash dealers must follow additional prudential rules to maintain financial stability; impacts data transfer risk assessments for financial data.

requirements.text: Cash dealers must comply with sections 7 and 10 of this Act
Cash dealers must comply with other prudential requirements specified by central bank

sections.source_quote: Every cash dealer shall at all times comply with such other prudential requirements as may be specified by the central bank.
sections.relevance_reason: prudential requirements for financial data

sections.heading: Restriction on payment of dividends
sections.title: Dividend payment restrictions
sections.summary: Banks cannot pay dividends or transfer profits abroad until the central bank confirms they won't violate capital adequacy or liquidity requirements, impair capital, or have adequate provisions for impaired credits. Bonus shares are treated as dividends.
sections.dtia_summary: Dividend restrictions ensure banks maintain financial stability before transferring funds; critical for DTIA of cross-border financial data flows.

requirements.text: Banks cannot pay dividends until central bank confirms payment won't violate capital adequacy or liquidity requirements
Banks cannot pay dividends until impairment in capital has been made good
Banks cannot pay dividends until adequate provisions for impaired credits are made
Bonus shares issued from profits are deemed dividends
Banks must make quarterly reports to central bank on dividend compliance matters

sections.source_quote: Notwithstanding the Companies Act 1984 and Companies Act 2001, no bank shall declare, credit or pay, or transfer abroad, any dividend or make any other transfer from profits until - (a) the central bank is satisfied that the payment of dividend or any other transfer from profits will not cause the bank to be in contravention of the capital adequacy requirements of section 20 or liquidity requirements of section 22, or likely to impair the future capital adequacy or liquidity of the bank.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: financial governance, not direct data compliance

sections.heading: Limitation on advances or credits
sections.title: Related party transaction limits
sections.summary: Banks and deposit-taking institutions cannot grant excessive advances to officers, employees, or affiliates without central bank approval. Transactions must be on terms comparable to third parties and not carry excessive risk.
sections.dtia_summary: Related party transactions require strict oversight to prevent conflicts of interest; impacts DTIA for cross-border financial data transfers involving related entities.

requirements.text: Banks cannot grant advances to officers exceeding annual emoluments
Banks cannot grant unsecured credits to employees exceeding annual emoluments
Banks cannot grant credits to affiliates exceeding central bank-determined limits
Central bank may determine maximum credit limits for related parties
Related party transactions must be on terms comparable to third parties
Related party transactions must not carry excessive risk

sections.source_quote: No bank or non-bank deposit taking institution shall - (a) grant any advance, or credit against the security of its own shares; (b) grant to, or permit to be outstanding from, its officers or employees unsecured advances or unsecured credit which, in the aggregate and in relation to any officer or employee, exceed the annual emoluments of that officer or employee;
sections.relevance_reason: Central bank oversight on financial transactions

sections.heading: Limitation on concentration of risk
sections.title: Regulatory credit concentration limits for banks
sections.summary: The central bank sets regulatory credit concentration limits for banks regarding individual large credit exposures to any one customer or group of closely-related customers, and aggregate large credit exposures to all such customers. The central bank may issue written instructions clarifying who constitutes a customer or group of closely-related customers. Any such instructions are binding on banks. The central bank may also exempt parts of a bank's banking or investment banking business conducted in currencies other than Mauritius currency from these concentration limits.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes credit concentration limits that affect cross-border data transfers by requiring banks to manage risks related to large exposures to specific customers or groups, which may impact data transfer assessments for international banking operations.

requirements.text: Central bank sets regulatory credit concentration limits for banks regarding individual large credit exposure to any one customer or group of closely-related customers
Central bank sets regulatory credit concentration limits for banks regarding aggregate large credit exposure to all customers and groups of closely-related customers

sections.source_quote: The central bank shall by way of guidelines set out regulatory credit concentration limits related to the capital base of a bank in respect of – (a) individual large credit exposure, including off balance sheet commitments, to any one customer or group of closely-related customers; and (b) aggregate amount of large credit exposure to all customers and groups of closely-related customers.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: financial risk management, not direct data handling

sections.heading: Limitation on investments and non-banking operations
sections.title: Restrictions on financial institutions' investments and non-banking activities
sections.summary: Financial institutions are prohibited from engaging in non-licensed activities except to satisfy debts due to them by default. They may only hold shares in specific entities approved by the central bank, including those promoting financial markets or economic development in Mauritius, up to 30% of their capital base. Banks must operate through subsidiaries for certain activities like factoring and securities brokerage. Banks cannot provide operating leases and may only hold assets in approved pools with central bank approval. Financial institutions cannot have significant interests in other banks in Mauritius.
sections.dtia_summary: This section restricts financial institutions' cross-border activities and investments, requiring central bank approval for certain international operations and asset holdings, which impacts data transfer impact assessments for international banking and financial services.

requirements.text: Financial institutions may not engage in non-licensed activities except to satisfy debts due to them by default

sections.heading: Acquisition of interest in a financial institution
sections.title: Acquisition of significant interest in financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions cannot allow any person to pledge or sell shares that would result in another person acquiring a significant interest without central bank approval. Any unauthorized sale or pledge of shares is invalid and void, causing the person to forfeit voting rights and dividends. Acquirers must provide detailed information to the central bank, including personal history, financial statements, and acquisition terms. The central bank may deny approvals if the acquisition would create undue influence, jeopardize financial stability, or not serve public interest.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs cross-border data transfers by requiring central bank approval for significant ownership changes in financial institutions, which impacts data transfer impact assessments for international financial services.

sections.heading: Mergers
sections.title: Bank mergers and transfers of undertaking
sections.summary: Financial institutions cannot merge or consolidate with other financial institutions without central bank approval. Banks must provide 30 days' notice before merging. The central bank evaluates financial and managerial resources, future prospects, and public convenience before approving mergers. Banks can transfer their undertaking to parent companies, subsidiaries, or other financial institutions with central bank approval, but must comply with specific conditions including asset and liability reporting and shareholder notification.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs cross-border data transfers by requiring central bank approval for bank mergers and transfers of undertaking, which impacts data transfer impact assessments for international banking operations.

requirements.text: Financial institutions cannot merge or consolidate with other financial institutions without central bank approval
Banks must provide 30 days' notice before merging

sections.heading: Mergers (cont.)
sections.title: Definition of assets, liabilities, and related terms for mergers
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in merger transactions, including 'assets' as property, rights, and privileges of the transferor bank, 'liabilities' as obligations of the transferor bank, and 'transferee bank' as the entity receiving the undertaking. It specifies that assets include personal character rights, non-assignable rights, trustee-held interests, and foreign law-based rights. Liabilities include personal character obligations and foreign law-based commitments. The section also defines 'parent', 'Registrar of Companies', and 'undertaking' as the whole or part of a business.

sections.heading: Records
sections.title: Financial institutions must maintain detailed records
sections.summary: Financial institutions must maintain comprehensive written records of all transactions, including accounting records, financial statements, customer account files, credit documentation, and other records as determined by the central bank. These records must be kept in written or electronic form for at least seven years after the transaction date, stored at the principal office or approved location, and organized chronologically for identification purposes.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial institutions must retain detailed records for seven years, including customer transaction data, which impacts data storage duration and location for cross-border transfers.

requirements.text: Every financial institution shall, for the purposes of the banking laws, keep in relation to its activities, a full and true written record of every transaction it conducts

the financial statements

account files of every customer, business correspondences exchanged with every customer and records showing, for every customer, at least on a daily basis, particulars of its transactions with or for the account of that customer, and the balance owing to or by that customer

for a period of at least 7 years after the completion of the transaction to which it relates
at the principal office of the financial institution, or at such other place as may be approved by the central bank
for identification purposes, in chronological order or sequential order, as appropriate, in batches of convenient size

sections.heading: Financial statements
sections.title: Financial institutions must prepare and submit audited financial statements
sections.summary: Financial institutions must prepare audited financial statements in accordance with International Accounting Standards and central bank guidelines, submit them to the central bank within three months of the financial year end, and publish them in the Gazette and on their website. Institutions with branches in Mauritius must also provide consolidated financial statements to the central bank within one month of publication.

requirements.text: Every financial institution shall, not later than 3 months after the end of its financial year, prepare, in accordance with the International Accounting Standards and such guidelines, not inconsistent with such Standards, as may be issued by the central bank, its audited financial statements for the financial year

sections.heading: Monthly statements
sections.title: Financial institutions must submit monthly statements to central bank
sections.summary: Financial institutions must submit monthly statements to the central bank showing the assets and liabilities of all offices and branches in Mauritius, along with an analysis of advances, bills discounted, and other credit activities. These statements must be provided within ten working days of the end of each month and may include additional information as required by the central bank for supervision purposes.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial institutions must submit monthly statements to the central bank, which impacts data storage and reporting requirements for cross-border financial operations.

requirements.text: Every financial institution shall, not later than the tenth working day of each month, forward to the central bank a statement, in such form and in such medium as may be approved by the central bank, showing the assets and liabilities of all its offices and branches in Mauritius together with an analysis of advances, bills discounted and any other credit as at the close of business on the last working day of the preceding month

Every financial institution shall furnish the central bank, within such period and in such manner as it may require, with such additional statements and information relating to the operations of the financial institution and those of its affiliates in Mauritius or its branches and affiliates outside Mauritius as the central bank may consider necessary or expedient to obtain for the purposes of the banking laws and for carrying out effective consolidated supervision

sections.source_quote: Every financial institution shall, not later than the tenth working day of each month, forward to the central bank a statement, in such form and in such medium as may be approved by the central bank, showing the assets and liabilities of all its offices and branches in Mauritius together with an analysis of advances, bills discounted and any other credit as at the close of business on the last working day of the preceding month.

sections.heading: Credit assessments and asset appraisals
sections.title: Central bank may require credit assessments and asset appraisals
sections.summary: The central bank may require banks to undergo independent credit worthiness assessments and asset valuations by approved third parties. Banks must provide results of these assessments and appraisals to the central bank, and authorized personnel have access to bank records for the purpose of conducting these evaluations.
sections.dtia_summary: Central bank may require credit assessments and asset appraisals, which impacts data sharing and third-party processing for financial institutions.

sections.heading: Disclosure of information
sections.title: Financial institutions must disclose credit information to customers
sections.summary: Financial institutions must disclose specific credit-related information to customers, including credit limits, interest rates, fees, minimum payments, and consequences of unauthorized card use. Banks must also provide detailed statements of account for credit facilities and notify customers of any changes to terms at least 30 days in advance.

sections.heading: Correction of disclosure statement
sections.title: Correction of incorrect financial disclosures
sections.summary: The central bank can require financial institutions to correct inaccurate or incomplete disclosure statements or quarterly reports by publishing revised statements or taking other corrective actions. This applies to financial institutions in Mauritius and covers both the correction of false information and the addition of required but omitted details. The central bank may specify the exact corrective actions needed in its notice.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to fix incorrect or missing disclosures before publishing, impacting data accuracy in cross-border financial reporting.

requirements.text: Publish a disclosure statement without incorrect, false or misleading information

sections.heading: Appointment, powers and duties of auditors
sections.title: Auditor appointment and responsibilities
sections.summary: Financial institutions must appoint auditors approved by the central bank, who must be independent, experienced in financial institution audits, and have sufficient resources. Auditors must prepare consolidated reports covering all entities, verify financial statements against international standards, and report on completeness, fairness, and compliance with prudential requirements. They must also report to the central bank on money laundering risks and other critical issues affecting financial stability.

sections.heading: Audit committee

sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish an audit committee with independent directors, which reviews financial statements, internal controls, and critical transactions. The committee must report to the board on audit matters and ensure compliance with banking laws and anti-money laundering requirements. The committee must also meet with internal and external auditors annually to maintain oversight.

sections.heading: Termination of services of auditor
sections.title: Termination of auditor services
sections.summary: Financial institutions must obtain central bank approval before terminating an auditor's services, including reasons for termination. sections.heading: Regular examinations
sections.title: Regular examinations by central bank
sections.summary: The central bank must conduct regular examinations of financial institutions at least every two years to assess compliance with banking laws, directives, and financial stability. These examinations may include affiliates and overseas branches, and costs can be recovered from financial institutions if necessary.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates periodic regulatory reviews to ensure financial institutions comply with data and financial reporting standards.

sections.heading: Special examinations
sections.title: Special examinations and enforcement
sections.summary: The central bank may conduct special examinations to assess financial stability and compliance with anti-money laundering regulations. It can require individuals to produce financial records, issue warnings to the public about unlicensed activities, and request police assistance for investigations.

sections.heading: Powers of examiners and special examiners
sections.title: Central bank examination powers for financial institutions
sections.summary: The central bank may authorise officers or persons to conduct regular or special examinations of financial institutions. These examiners can access all books, records, cash, securities, and electronic data held by the institution or its affiliates in Mauritius or overseas. They may also require information and copies of relevant documents within a specified timeframe. All appointed persons must comply with confidentiality obligations under banking laws.
sections.dtia_summary: This section grants regulators broad access to financial institution records and data across borders, requiring DTIAs to address cross-border data access and examination powers during assessments.

requirements.text: Central bank may authorise officers or persons to conduct regular or special examinations of financial institutions

Examiners may access electronic data and take extracts from computer or electronic devices of financial institutions or affiliates in Mauritius or overseas
Examiners may require information and copies of relevant documents within specified timeframe

sections.source_quote: The central bank may authorise in writing any of its officers or any other person appointed by the central bank for that purpose to conduct, either jointly or separately, a regular examination under section 42 or a special examination under section 43, and any such officer or person shall have the power to – (a) examine all books, minutes, accounts, records, cash, securities, vouchers and any other document, in the possession or custody of the financial institution or of its affiliates in Mauritius or its branches and affiliates outside Mauritius; (aa) have access to any program or data and take extracts of any file, document or record held electronically in any computer or other electronic device of the financial institution or of its affiliates in Mauritius or its branches and affiliates outside Mauritius; and (b) require, within such time as may be specified, such information and copies of all relevant documents, that he may reasonably require concerning its business, or that of its affiliates in Mauritius, or that of its branches and affiliates outside Mauritius, if any, as appear necessary.

sections.heading: Powers of central bank following examination
sections.title: Central bank enforcement powers after financial institution examination
sections.summary: If the central bank finds a financial institution unsafe or unsound, it may impose conditions on its license, require remedial steps, or appoint advisors. The central bank can also issue cease and desist orders, suspend officers, or freeze assets. All actions must be notified in writing to the institution and relevant parties, with opportunities for hearings within 14-15 days before final decisions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section enables regulators to enforce cross-border data and asset controls during financial examinations, requiring DTIAs to address potential data freezes and asset restrictions in international contexts.

requirements.text: Central bank may impose or vary conditions on financial institution's license if it is unsafe or unsound
Central bank may require financial institution to take steps to remedy unsafe or unsound conditions
Central bank may appoint advisors to guide financial institution's business conduct
Central bank may issue cease and desist orders requiring cessation of unsafe practices
Central bank may suspend directors or senior officers from office
Suspension of directors may be extended or made permanent after hearing
Central bank must notify financial institution and relevant parties of actions taken
Recipient of notice may request hearing within 15 days
Central bank must provide hearing opportunity within 14 days of request
Central bank must render decision within 15 days of hearing completion

sections.source_quote: Where, in relation to any financial institution, the central bank is of the opinion that as a result of an examination made under section 42 or a special examination made under section 43 or other information at its disposal, that - (a) any director or senior officer or employee of the financial institution is not a fit and proper person; (b) the financial institution has, or any of its directors or senior officers or employees have, engaged in unsafe or unsound practices in conducting its business in a manner detrimental to the interests of its depositors, or the financial institution has, or has knowingly or negligently permitted any of its directors or senior officers or employees or agents to violate any provision of the banking laws or any enactment relating to anti-money laundering or prevention of terrorism and the regulations, directives, guidelines, or instructions issued by the central bank to which the financial institution is subject, or the central bank has reasonable cause to believe that such actions or violations are about to occur; (c) the financial institution has insufficient assets to cover its liabilities; (d) the amount paid as stated capital or the assigned capital, as the case may be, of the financial institution is impaired; or (e) the financial institution is otherwise in an unsafe or unsound condition, the central bank may - (i) impose or vary conditions attaching to the financial institution's licence in accordance with section 10 or 16, as the case may be, invoking in cases of urgency the procedure specified in section 17; (ii) require the financial institution forthwith to take such steps as may appear to the central bank to be necessary to remedy the situation; and (iii) appoint a person to advise the financial institution in the proper conduct of its business and fix the remuneration to be paid by the financial institution to the person so appointed.

sections.heading: Fit and proper person
sections.title: Fit and proper person requirements for financial institution officers
sections.summary: Financial institutions must ensure directors and senior officers are fit and proper by following central bank guidelines. Appointments require 20 days' notice, certificates of good conduct, and central bank approval. The central bank evaluates candidates based on probity, integrity, competence, business experience, and absence of fraud convictions. Non-compliance triggers suspension orders.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes fit and proper criteria for financial officers, requiring DTIAs to assess whether data processing activities comply with these standards for cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions.

No financial institution may appoint senior officers without 20 days' notice to central bank
Appointments require certificate of good conduct or affidavit stating convictions
Central bank must be satisfied person is fit and proper before appointment
Central bank must communicate objections within 15 days of notification
Central bank may suspend person who attempts appointment over objection
Central bank may require financial institutions to implement conflict of interest policies

sections.source_quote: No person shall be appointed or reappointed as director of a financial institution unless the appointment or reappointment takes into account the guidelines issued by the central bank relating to fit and proper persons. sections.heading: Disqualification
sections.title: Disqualification of financial institution officers
sections.summary: Directors and senior officers who are bankrupt or convicted of fraud/dishonesty must cease holding office. No person may simultaneously hold director positions at multiple financial institutions without central bank approval. Former directors of liquidated institutions cannot act as directors without central bank approval.
sections.dtia_summary: This section restricts officer eligibility for financial institutions, requiring DTIAs to verify that data processing activities comply with disqualification rules for cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions.

sections.heading: Disclosure of interest
sections.title: Conflict of interest disclosure for financial institution officers
sections.summary: Directors and senior officers must disclose any financial interests or potential conflicts of interest to the board and abstain from decisions related to those interests. sections.heading: Indemnity insurance
sections.title: Indemnity insurance requirements for financial institutions
sections.summary: The central bank may require financial institutions to provide indemnity insurance against burglary, defalcation, and other insurable losses. This ensures financial institutions have protection against physical and financial risks.

sections.heading: Automated teller machines
sections.title: Automated teller machine setup and security requirements
sections.summary: Banks must inform the central bank when setting up ATMs for customer deposits or withdrawals. Banks must implement security measures including customer authentication systems, terminal receipts, periodic statements, and physical/logical protections against unauthorized access. These requirements apply to all banks operating ATMs in Mauritius.

sections.heading: Clearing house and payments system

sections.summary: Banks must comply with central bank instructions for the smooth functioning of the clearing house and payments system including MACSS. Every financial institution and every holder of a licence shall, in relation to the development of new products and new business practices, including new delivery mechanisms, and the use of new or developing technologies for both new and pre-existing products – (a) undertake a risk assessment prior to the launch or use of such products, business practices and technologies; (b) identify and assess the money laundering and terrorism financing risks that may arise in relation to the launch or use of such products, business practices and technologies; and (c) take appropriate measures to manage and mitigate the risks identified.

sections.heading: Internal control systems
sections.title: Internal control systems and whistleblower protection
sections.summary: Financial institutions must maintain adequate internal control systems covering operations, accounting, risk measurement, documentation, and cash flow monitoring. False disclosures or victimisation of whistleblowers are punishable by fines up to 1 million rupees and imprisonment up to 5 years.

sections.heading: Identity of customers
sections.title: Customer identity verification requirements
sections.summary: Financial institutions must verify the true identity of account holders or safe deposit box lessees before opening accounts. Accounts must be properly named to ensure the true owner can be identified by the public without misleading names.

Every financial institution shall require that each of its accounts be properly named, at all times, so that the true owner of the accounts can be identified by the public and no name shall be allowed that is likely to mislead the public.

sections.summary: Financial institutions must provide customers with written or electronic statements showing cheque payments and paid cheques, including sufficient transaction details and fee information. Statements must be sent at least annually, with inactive accounts potentially ceasing statement delivery after three undelivered statements. Banks must also display fee rates prominently and online.

sections.heading: Abandoned funds

sections.heading: Confidentiality

sections.source_quote: 69. (2) Where the Board authorises the conservator to proceed to reorganise the financial institution, the conservator shall, after granting a hearing to all interested parties, propose a reorganisation plan and send a copy of it to all depositors and other creditors who shall not receive full payment under the plan. (3) The copy of the reorganisation plan shall be accompanied by a notice stating that where the reorganisation plan is not refused in writing within a period of 30 days by persons holding not less than one-third of the aggregate amount of deposits and creditors comprising not less than one third in value of the aggregate of the claims of creditors other than subordinated creditors, the conservator shall, with the approval of the Board, proceed to carry out the reorganisation plan. (3A) Where the reorganisation plan is refused in writing within a period of 30 days by persons holding not less than one third of the aggregate amount of deposits and creditors comprising not less than one third in value of the aggregate of the claims of creditors other than subordinated creditors, the conservator may, subject to subsection (3), propose another reorganisation plan to all depositors and other creditors who shall not receive full payment under the plan. (4) The approval of a reorganisation plan by the Board shall be subject to its finding that the reorganisation plan shall - (a) be equitable under the circumstances, to depositors, other creditors and shareholders; (b) provide for bringing in new funds so as to establish adequate ratios between - (i) capital and deposits; (ii) capital and risk assets; (iii) liquid assets and deposits; and (c) provide for the removal of any director, chief executive officer, manager, officer or employee responsible for the circumstances which necessitated the appointment of the conservator.

sections.title: Civil and criminal liability for financial institution misconduct
sections.summary: The conservator, receiver, or central bank may initiate civil actions against directors, officers, employees, or contractors for gross negligence or intentional wrongdoing causing financial harm. They may also recommend criminal prosecution to the Director of Public Prosecutions.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides legal recourse for misconduct, which could affect data transfers if international entities are involved in the misconduct.

requirements.text: Initiate civil actions against directors, officers, employees, or contractors for gross negligence or intentional wrongdoing
Recommend criminal prosecution to Director of Public Prosecutions for misconduct

sections.source_quote: The conservator or receiver or the central bank may bring a civil action against any director, chief executive officer, manager, officer, employee, agent or independent contractor of a financial institution for gross negligence or intentional wrong for damages caused to that financial institution and may recommend to the Director of Public Prosecutions the criminal prosecution of any such person.
sections.relevance_reason: Civil liability for misconduct covers breach notification and security duties

sections.heading: Winding up of financial institutions
sections.title: Winding up procedures for financial institutions
sections.summary: Winding up of financial institutions follows the Insolvency Act 2009 and its regulations unless Parts X and XI apply. Depositor balances are presumed valid unless the liquidator doubts the entries.
sections.dtia_summary: Uses standard insolvency procedures for winding up, which may impact data transfers if international depositors are involved.

requirements.text: Apply Insolvency Act 2009 and regulations for winding up unless Parts X and XI apply
Presume depositor balances as valid unless liquidator doubts the entries

sections.source_quote: The provisions of Sub-Part II of Part III, Part IV and Part VII of the Insolvency Act 2009 and such regulations as may be prescribed shall apply in relation to the winding up of a financial institution, where the provisions of Parts X and XI are not resorted to, or any of the provisions of those Parts are not otherwise applicable. The amounts shown in the books of a financial institution as standing to the credit of depositors shall, unless the liquidator shows that there is reason to doubt the entry, be presumed to be proof of those amounts without further proof from the depositors.
sections.relevance_reason: Winding up procedures affect data handling during insolvency

sections.heading: Priority of deposit liabilities
sections.title: Priority of deposit liabilities in insolvency
sections.summary: When a financial institution becomes insolvent or unable to meet obligations in Mauritius, its assets must first satisfy deposit liabilities of customers in Mauritius. These deposit liabilities take priority over all unsecured liabilities except those specified in the Insolvency Act 2009 as having priority claims. This ensures depositors are protected before other creditors.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes priority for deposit liabilities over other unsecured liabilities during insolvency, which impacts data transfer DTIAs by requiring financial institutions to prioritize customer data protection in cross-border insolvency scenarios.

requirements.text: Deposit liabilities of a financial institution in Mauritius shall have priority over all unsecured liabilities except those specified in the Insolvency Act 2009

sections.source_quote: Subject to section 92, where a financial institution becomes unable to meet its obligations or becomes insolvent or suspends payment, the assets of the financial institution in Mauritius shall be available to meet all deposit liabilities of the financial institution in Mauritius, and those deposit liabilities shall have priority over all unsecured liabilities of the financial institution other than those expenses and debts specified in the Insolvency Act 2009 to have priority of claim over all other liabilities of the company in the event of a winding up.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: insolvency priority for deposits

sections.heading: Priority of deposit and other liabilities in case of winding up of a bank and non-
sections.title: Priority order for deposit liabilities in insolvency
sections.summary: In the winding up of a bank or non-bank deposit taking institution, deposit liabilities must be settled in a specific priority order: first those with non-bank customers, then those with other banks, and finally other liabilities. Each class of liabilities is paid in full unless assets are insufficient, in which case they are settled proportionally.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the priority order for deposit liabilities during insolvency, which affects DTIAs by requiring financial institutions to ensure customer data is protected before other data transfers or processing activities in cross-border insolvency scenarios.

requirements.text: Deposit liabilities of a bank and non-bank deposit taking institution shall be settled in the order: (a) with non-bank customers; (b) with other banks; (c) other liabilities
Each class of deposit liabilities shall rank equally and be paid in full unless assets are insufficient, in which case they are settled proportionally
Deposit liabilities include sums of money repaid with or without interest, on demand or at a time agreed, not referable to property or services or security
Money paid for property or services or as security shall not be considered deposit liabilities

sections.source_quote: All assets of the bank and non-bank deposit taking institution shall be available to meet all deposit liabilities of the bank and non-bank deposit taking institution in the following order of priority - (a) deposit liabilities incurred by the bank and non-bank deposit taking institution with non-bank and non-bank deposit taking institution customers; (b) deposit liabilities incurred by the bank and non-bank deposit taking institution with other banks and non-bank deposit taking institutions; (c) other liabilities of the bank and non-bank deposit taking institution.
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant to data compliance

sections.heading: Deposit insurance scheme
sections.title: Deposit insurance scheme for financial institutions
sections.summary: A deposit insurance scheme must be established to protect depositors against losses in the event of bank failure, contributing to financial system stability. The scheme is funded through premiums levied on banks and covers specified types of deposits up to a defined ceiling. The central bank may provide funds to the insurance fund as needed.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates a deposit insurance scheme that impacts DTIAs by requiring financial institutions to ensure customer data is protected through insurance mechanisms in cross-border financial operations.

requirements.text: A deposit insurance scheme must be established to provide insurance against loss of deposits in a bank
The scheme shall be funded through a deposit insurance fund with premiums levied on banks and costs associated with bank restructuring or liquidation
The scheme must specify types of deposits covered and the coverage ceiling
The central bank may advance funds to the deposit insurance fund on repayment terms as it deems fit

sections.source_quote: There shall be established and maintained, in such manner as may be prescribed, a deposit insurance scheme to provide insurance against the loss of part or all of deposits in a bank in a manner that will contribute to the stability of the financial system in Mauritius and minimize the exposure to loss.
sections.relevance_reason: Mauritius banking act focuses on financial stability, not data protection

sections.heading: Derogations from articles 1659, 1660, 1661, 1673, 2087 and 2088 of the Code
sections.title: Derogations from civil code for repurchase transactions
sections.summary: Repurchase transactions involving government securities, Bank of Mauritius Bills, or other specified instruments between banks and financial institutions are exempt from certain provisions of the Civil Code Mauricien. The central bank may specify terms for these repurchase transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects DTIAs by exempting specific repurchase transactions from civil code provisions, which may impact data transfer mechanisms for financial institutions.

requirements.text: Articles 1659, 1660, 1661 and 1673 of the Civil Code Mauricien do not apply to repurchase transactions involving government securities, Bank of Mauritius Bills, or other specified instruments
Articles 2087 and 2088 of the Civil Code Mauricien do not apply to securities given for repurchase of instruments referred to above
The central bank shall specify terms and conditions for repurchase transactions

sections.source_quote: Pursuant to the second alinea of article 2094 of the Code Civil Mauricien and notwithstanding any other enactment - articles 1659, 1660, 1661 and 1673 of the Code Civil Mauricien shall not apply to commercial contracts involving purchases made with a provision for repurchase of - (i) Government securities; (ii) Bank of Mauritius Bills; or (iii) such other instruments as the central bank may specify, among banks and such other financial institutions as the central bank may specify; and articles 2087 and 2088 of the Code Civil Mauricien shall not apply to securities given for the repurchase of instruments referred to in paragraph (a).
sections.relevance_reason: Central bank terms for financial transactions

sections.heading: Immunity
sections.title: Immunity for government and central bank actions
sections.summary: No legal action can be brought against the Government, the central bank, its officers, or employees for actions taken in good faith in administering this Act or executing powers relevant to this Act. This immunity protects government and central bank officials from liability for financial institution regulatory actions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides immunity for government and central bank actions, which may impact DTIAs by ensuring regulatory actions related to data transfers are protected from legal challenges.

requirements.text: No action shall lie against the Government, the central bank, any officer or employee of the central bank or any person acting under the direction of the central bank for anything done or omitted to be done in good faith in the administration of this Act

sections.source_quote: No action shall lie against the Government, the central bank, any officer or employee of the central bank or any person acting under the direction of the central bank for anything done or omitted to be done in good faith in the administration of this Act, or in the execution of any powers or duties authorised or required under any other enactment that are relevant to this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Government immunity irrelevant to data compliance

sections.heading: Repealed
sections.title: Customer complaint handling for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must appoint an officer to handle customer complaints and grievances. Customers can make written complaints within 7 years of the incident, and the institution must resolve complaints within the timeframe specified in the Ombudsperson for Financial Services Act 2018. Complaints can be referred to the Ombudsperson if unresolved.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts DTIAs by requiring financial institutions to establish complaint handling mechanisms for customer data, which affects data transfer and processing activities.

requirements.text: Every financial institution (other than a cash dealer) shall appoint an officer to deal with complaints and grievances from customers
Customers may make written complaints within 7 years of the incident
Financial institutions must resolve complaints within the timeframe specified in the Ombudsperson for Financial Services Act 2018
Complaints can be referred to the Ombudsperson for Financial Services if unresolved

sections.source_quote: Every financial institution, other than a cash dealer, shall appoint an officer to deal with complaints and grievances from their customers.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: complaint handling not directly data-related

sections.title: Repealed provision for interest rate regulations
sections.summary: This section states that the provision was repealed. The central bank may issue instructions or guidelines necessary for the purposes of this section. The section does not contain any operative obligations or conditions for financial institutions regarding interest rates or loan agreements.

requirements.text: The central bank may issue such instructions or guidelines as may be necessary for the purposes of this section

sections.source_quote: The central bank may issue such instructions or guidelines as may be necessary for the purposes of this section.
sections.relevance_reason: Repealed provision with no data compliance impact

sections.heading: Offences and penalties
sections.title: Offences and penalties for banking violations
sections.summary: This section outlines various offences and penalties for non-compliance with banking regulations. Financial institutions and individuals who fail to obtain necessary licenses, comply with central bank requirements, or engage in prohibited activities face fines and imprisonment. Specific penalties apply for offences related to license display, document falsification, and failure to follow central bank directives.

requirements.text: Any person who transacts banking business without a licence or written authorisation from the central bank shall commit an offence and be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and imprisonment for up to 5 years
Any person who fails to comply with the central bank's requirements under section 43(2) shall commit an offence and be liable to a fine not exceeding 500,000 rupees and imprisonment for up to 2 years
Any person who hinders the central bank in special examinations shall commit an offence and be liable to a fine not exceeding 100,000 rupees per day and imprisonment for up to 2 years

Any person who furnishes false or misleading information in banking licence applications shall commit an offence and be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and imprisonment for up to 5 years
Financial institutions failing to display their licence or comply with section 57(7) or (8) shall commit an offence and be liable to a fine of 10,000-50,000 rupees per day
Financial institutions opening new branches without central bank approval shall commit an offence
Unlicensed persons using terms like 'bank' or 'deposit taking' in their activities shall commit an offence
Directors or senior officers failing to comply with section 48 shall commit an offence and be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and imprisonment for up to 5 years
Financial institutions failing to take corrective actions under section 38 shall commit an offence and be liable to a fine of 10,000-50,000 rupees per day
Financial institutions failing to produce required documents under section 44 shall commit an offence and be liable to a fine of 10,000-50,000 rupees per day
Financial institutions providing false information under section 44 shall commit an offence and be liable to a fine of 1 million-5 million rupees
Financial institutions failing to take measures specified by the central bank under section 45(1)(ii) shall commit an offence and be liable to a fine of 500,000 rupees per day
Financial institutions failing to cease or desist from actions specified in a cease and desist order shall commit an offence and be liable to a fine of 5 million rupees

sections.source_quote: Any person who transacts banking business without a licence or written authorisation from the central bank shall commit an offence and be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and imprisonment for up to 5 years.
sections.relevance_reason: Penalties for banking violations

sections.heading: Prosecution for offence
sections.title: Prosecution for offences
sections.summary: This section specifies that prosecutions for offences under the Act must be initiated by or with the consent of the Director of Public Prosecutions. It establishes the legal authority for initiating criminal proceedings against individuals or entities found guilty of violations of the banking regulations.

requirements.text: No prosecution for an offence under this Act shall be instituted except by or with the consent of the Director of Public Prosecutions

sections.source_quote: No prosecution for an offence under this Act or any regulations made thereunder shall be instituted except by or with the consent of the Director of Public Prosecutions.
sections.relevance_reason: irrelevant: prosecution process, not data compliance

sections.heading: Compounding of offences
sections.title: Compounding of offences
sections.summary: This section allows the central bank to compound offences with the consent of the Director of Public Prosecutions. Offenders who agree to pay an amount not exceeding the maximum penalty specified for the offence can avoid further legal proceedings. The compounding agreement is final and conclusive upon payment.

requirements.text: The central bank may compound any offence committed by a person under this Act with the consent of the Director of Public Prosecutions where the person agrees in writing to pay an amount not exceeding the maximum penalty specified for the offence
Every agreement to compound shall be final and conclusive and on payment of the agreed amount, no further proceedings in regard to the offence shall be taken against the person

sections.source_quote: The central bank may, with the consent of the Director of Public Prosecutions, compound any offence committed by a person under this Act which is prescribed as a compoundable offence, where the person agrees in writing to pay such amount not exceeding the maximum penalty specified for the offence, acceptable to the central bank.
sections.relevance_reason: Compounding offences unrelated to data compliance

sections.heading: Directives, guidelines or instructions
sections.title: Directives, guidelines or instructions
sections.summary: This section empowers the central bank to issue directives, guidelines, or instructions for financial institutions. These directives apply to specific categories of financial institutions and take effect upon issuance or a later date specified in the directives. Non-compliance with these directives results in fines and imprisonment.

requirements.text: The central bank may make such directives, guidelines or instructions as it thinks fit for the purposes of this Act

Directives, guidelines or instructions made under this section shall apply to all financial institutions or one or more categories of financial institutions and take effect on the date of issue or a later date specified in the directives
The central bank may issue specific instructions to any financial institution which take effect on the date of issue or a later date specified in the instructions

The central bank may issue guidelines, instructions or directives to ultimate and intermediate financial holding companies in Mauritius with at least one subsidiary or joint venture that is a bank or non-bank deposit-taking institution
The central bank may require ultimate and intermediate financial holding companies to comply with specific provisions of this Act to ensure effective supervision
Any person to whom directives, guidelines or instructions are issued shall comply with those directives, guidelines and instructions
Any person who fails to comply with directives, guidelines or instructions shall commit an offence and be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and imprisonment for up to 2 years

sections.source_quote: The central bank may make such directives, guidelines or instructions as it thinks fit for the purposes of this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Central bank directives on financial data compliance

sections.heading: Regulations
sections.title: Regulations for the Act
sections.summary: This section authorizes the Minister to make regulations for the purposes of the Act. Regulations may prescribe compoundable offences, amend schedules, and provide for fees and charges. The central bank may also make regulations for processing or licence fees under specific sections and to discharge obligations binding on Mauritius by UN Security Council decisions.

requirements.text: The Minister may make such regulations as he thinks fit for the purposes of this Act
The Minister may by regulations prescribe offences which shall be compoundable offences for the purposes of section 99
The Minister may by regulations amend the Schedules, other than the Third Schedule
Regulations may provide for the payment of fees and the levying of charges
Regulations may provide that any person who contravenes them shall commit an offence and be liable to a fine not exceeding 200,000 rupees and imprisonment for up to 3 years
The central bank may, with the approval of the Minister, make regulations for prescribing processing fees or licence fees under section 7(7A) or 11A(3)(a) and (c)
The central bank may make regulations to discharge or facilitate the discharge of any obligation binding on Mauritius by virtue of a decision of the United Nations Security Council

sections.source_quote: The Minister may make such regulations as he thinks fit for the purposes of this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory powers for financial data handling

sections.heading: Transitional provision
sections.title: Capital requirements for banks licensed before 2017
sections.summary: Banks licensed prior to the Finance (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2017's section 4(b) commencement must raise capital to at least 300 million rupees by June 30, 2018, and 400 million rupees by June 30, 2019. This transitional provision sets specific capital thresholds for existing banks to meet before the new act takes effect.

requirements.text: Banks licensed before section 4(b) of the Finance (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2017 commencement must raise capital to not less than 300 million rupees by 30 June 2018
Banks licensed before section 4(b) of the Finance (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2017 commencement must raise capital to not less than 400 million rupees by 30 June 2019

sections.source_quote: 102. Transitional provision. Any bank licensed before the commencement of section 4(b) of the Finance (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2017 shall, on the commencement of that section, raise its capital to not less than – (a) 300 million rupees by 30 June 2018; and (b) 400 million rupees by 30 June 2019.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure financial regulation, no data privacy aspects

sections.heading: Repeal and savings
sections.title: Repeal and savings provisions for banking acts
sections.summary: This section repeals the Banking Act and Foreign Exchange Dealers Act, but preserves existing licenses, approvals, and authorizations issued under those acts. It ensures that all licenses, fees, and proceedings remain valid under the new act, with specific provisions for banking licenses and guidelines.

requirements.text: The Banking Act and Foreign Exchange Dealers Act are repealed
Licenses, certificates, approvals, and authorizations issued under repealed acts remain valid for their specified periods
Category 1 and Category 2 Banking Licenses issued under repealed acts are deemed valid under this Act
Licence fees paid under repealed acts are deemed paid under this Act for the remaining period
Guidelines or instructions issued by the central bank under repealed acts remain valid under this Act
Proceedings commenced and pending under repealed acts continue under this Act
Acts or things done under repealed acts are deemed done under this Act

sections.source_quote: 104. Repeal and savings. (1) The following enactments are repealed - (a) The Banking Act; and (b) The Foreign Exchange Dealers Act. (2) Notwithstanding the repeal of the enactments specified in subsection (1) - (a) any licence or certificate issued, any approval given or authorisation granted under the repealed enactments and in force on the date immediately before the coming into operation of this Act shall be deemed to have been issued, given or granted under this Act and any such licence, certificate, approval or authorisation shall remain valid for the period specified therein; (b) any Category 1 Banking Licence or Category 2 Banking Licence issued under the repealed enactment referred to in subsection (1)(a) shall, on the commencement of this Act, be deemed to be a banking licence issued under this Act; (c) any licence fee paid under the repealed enactments referred to in subsection (1) shall, on the commencement of this Act, be deemed to have been paid under this Act for the remaining period to which it applies; (d) any guidelines or instructions issued by the central bank under the repealed enactments and in force on the date immediately before the coming into operation of this Act shall be deemed to have been issued under this Act; (e) all proceedings, judicial or otherwise under the repealed enactments commenced before and pending on the date immediately before the coming into operation of this Act shall be deemed to have been commenced and may be continued under this Act; (f) any act or thing done under the repealed enactments shall be deemed to have been done under this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Repeal of acts, no data handling

sections.heading: Commencement
sections.title: Commencement dates for the Banking Act
sections.summary: This section allows the Act to come into effect on a date set by Proclamation, with the possibility of different commencement dates for different provisions. It specifies that the Act's operation is subject to a proclamation and that different provisions may have different effective dates.

requirements.text: This Act shall come into operation on a date to be fixed by Proclamation
Different dates may be fixed for the coming into operation of different provisions of this Act

sections.source_quote: 105. Commencement. (1) Subject to subsection (2), this Act shall come into operation on a date to be fixed by Proclamation. (2) Different dates may be fixed for the coming into operation of different provisions of this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: commencement dates irrelevant to compliance

sections.heading: Where the central bank authorises the transfer of the whole or part of the undertaking
sections.title: Asset transfer upon banking undertaking transfer
sections.summary: When a bank transfers its undertaking to another bank under section 32A, all assets and liabilities listed in the final list of assets and liabilities submitted to the Registrar-General vest in the transferee bank without further formality. The transferor bank is released from liabilities related to the transferred undertaking, and pending proceedings continue with the transferee bank.

requirements.text: All assets and liabilities that form part of the undertaking of the transferor bank on the day immediately before the appointed day and are listed in the final list of assets and liabilities submitted to the Registrar-General under section 32A(8) shall vest in the transferee bank on the appointed day
The transferor bank shall be released from all liabilities and obligations that form part of its undertaking on the day immediately before the appointed day
All proceedings started and pending by or against the transferor bank that form part of the undertaking shall be continued by or against the transferee bank on the appointed day
Any ruling, order or judgment in favour of or against the transferor bank in respect of the undertaking shall be enforceable by or against the transferee bank on the appointed day

Any mortgage, privilege, charge, bond, pledge, guarantee or security interest that form part of the undertaking shall remain in full force and be considered a mortgage, privilege, bond, pledge, guarantee or instrument given to the transferee bank
Every book, record and other document of the transferor bank that form part of its undertaking shall vest in the transferee bank on the appointed day

sections.source_quote: 1. Where the central bank authorises the transfer of the whole or part of the undertaking of a bank under section 32A – (a) all assets and liabilities that form part of the undertaking of the transferor bank on the day immediately before the appointed day and are listed in the final list of assets and liabilities submitted to the Registrar-General under section 32A(8), shall, on the appointed day, vest in and continue to be the assets and liabilities of the transferee bank, without the need for any further formality; (b) the transferor bank shall, in respect of the transferred undertaking, on the appointed day, be released from all liabilities and obligations that form part of its undertaking on the day immediately before the appointed day; (c) all proceedings started and pending by or against the transferor bank that form part of the undertaking of the transferor bank on the day immediately before the appointed day shall, in respect of the transferred undertaking, on the appointed day, be continued by or against the transferee bank, and the transferee bank shall, in respect of the transferred undertaking, be entitled to all defences, claims, counterclaims and rights of set-off or compensation which would have been available to the transferor bank in relation to such proceedings; (d) any ruling, order or judgment in favour of or against the transferor bank in respect of the undertaking of the transferor bank on the day immediately before the appointed day shall, in respect of the transferred undertaking, on the appointed day, be enforceable by or against the transferee bank; (e) any mortgage, privilege, charge, bond, pledge, guarantee or any other security interest that form part of the undertaking of the transferor bank on the day immediately before the appointed day and was made or given to secure past, present and future advances, facilities or services by the transferor bank shall, in respect of the transferred undertaking, on the appointed day, remain in full force and be considered to be a mortgage, privilege, bond, pledge, guarantee or instrument given to or in favour of the transferee bank, as security for past, present and future advances, facilities or services by the transferee bank, without the need for any further formality; (f) every book, record and other document of the transferor bank that form part of its undertaking on the day immediately before the appointed day shall, in respect of the transferred undertaking, on the appointed day, vest in the transferee bank.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure amendment mechanics

sections.heading: (a)
sections.title: Employee transfer upon banking undertaking transfer
sections.summary: Employees of the transferor bank who are transferred to the transferee bank must receive terms and conditions not less favourable than their previous employment. Their period of service is deemed unbroken with the transferee bank, and they cannot claim termination or adverse effects due to the transfer. Pension schemes operated by the transferor bank for employees on the day before the appointed day are transferred to the transferee bank.

requirements.text: Every person employed by the transferor bank on the day immediately before the appointed day shall be entitled to be transferred to the transferee bank on terms and conditions not less favourable than those of his previous employment
The period of service of every person employed by the transferor bank who is transferred to the transferee bank shall be deemed to be an unbroken period of service with the transferee bank
No person employed by the transferor bank shall be entitled to claim that his employment has been terminated or adversely affected in breach of any enactment due to transfer to the transferee bank

Any pension or similar scheme operated by the transferor bank for the benefit of its employees on the day immediately before the appointed day shall be operated by the transferee bank as from the appointed day

sections.source_quote: 2. (a) Every person who, in relation to the transferred undertaking, on the day immediately before the appointed day, is employed by the transferor bank shall be entitled to be transferred to the transferee bank on terms and conditions which shall be not less favourable than those of his previous employment at the transferor bank. (b) The period of service of every person employed by the transferor bank who is transferred to the transferee bank shall be deemed to be an unbroken period of service with the transferee bank. (c) No person employed by the transferor bank shall, on account of his transfer to the transferee bank or any resulting change in his job title, be entitled to claim that his employment has been terminated or adversely affected in breach of any enactment. (d) Any pension or similar scheme operated by the transferor bank for the benefit of its employees on the day immediately before the appointed day shall, as from the appointed day, be operated by the transferee bank.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: employee transfer rules not directly data-related

sections.heading: (a)
sections.title: Shareholder transfer upon banking undertaking transfer
sections.summary: Shareholders of the transferor bank on the day before the appointed day are considered to hold shares in the transferee bank representing their proportionate share of the net asset value of the transferred undertaking. Shareholders may exercise their right to require the transferor bank to purchase their shares under section 32A(5). The right to cease being a shareholder may be exercised by shareholders before or after the appointed day.

requirements.text: Every shareholder of the transferor bank on the day immediately before the appointed day shall be considered to hold in the transferee bank a number of shares representing his proportionate share of the net asset value that the transferred undertaking bears to the net asset value of the transferee bank following the completion of the transfer
Shareholders may exercise their right to require the transferor bank to purchase their shares in accordance with section 32A(5)
Shareholders other than the Government may cease to be shareholders of the transferor bank immediately before the appointed day
Shareholders may cease to be shareholders of the transferee bank on or after the appointed day

sections.source_quote: 3. (a) Subject to paragraph (b), every person who is a shareholder of the transferor bank on the day immediately before the appointed day – (i) shall, on the appointed day, be considered to hold in the transferee bank a number of shares representing his proportionate share of the net asset value that the transferred undertaking bears to the net asset value of the transferee bank following the completion of the transfer; or (ii) may exercise his right to require the transferor bank to purchase his shares in accordance with section 32A(5). (b) Paragraph (a) shall be without prejudice to the right of any shareholder, other than the Government, to cease to be a shareholder – (i) of the transferor bank immediately before the appointed day; or (ii) of the transferee bank on or after the appointed day.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure amendment mechanics, no data compliance relevance
